mainEntity.name: Why does my daughter hits her head. not once in a while — repeatedly, intensely, rhythmically, sometimes for minutes at a time, sometimes until there are bruises or welts or worse. the first time i saw it, i thought my heart would stop. i grabbed her hands, i begged her to stop, i cried. nothing helped. she fought against my grip. she found other ways to hit when i held her hands. she seemed more distressed by my intervention than by the hitting itself. it kept happening. when she's frustrated because she can't communicate what she wants. when she's overwhelmed by too much sensory input or too many demands. when there's a change in routine she wasn't prepared for. when something hurts inside her body — maybe her ears, maybe her stomach, maybe something else — and she can't name or locate the pain. when she's bored and understimulated. when she's excited and can't contain the energy. when she's tired. when she's hungry. it seems like everything leads here eventually. her hands go to her head, and she hits. over and over, with a force that makes me wince, with a rhythm that seems almost automatic. i've tried everything i can think of. holding her hands down — she fights harder, or she bites her arm instead, or she bangs her head on the floor. the self-injury finds a way. ignoring it — i physically cannot ignore my child hurting herself, and even when i've tried, it doesn't stop, it escalates until she gets a response. punishment — she doesn't seem to register consequences when she's in that state, and punishing her afterward feels cruel and pointless. rewards for not hitting — but she's not choosing to hit, so how can she choose not to? pleading, reasoning, explaining — words don't reach her when she's there. it's like she's not in a place where language works. i've padded surfaces. i've bought helmets she won't wear. i've hovered constantly, hands ready to intercept. i've cried more tears than i knew i had. the doctors say it's self-injurious behavior. sib. they use the clinical term like it explains something, but it doesn't explain why my baby hurts herself. they say the behavior is functional — that sounds wrong until they explain it. functional means it works. it serves a purpose. it gets her something her brain desperately needs. maybe it's sensory input — deep pressure, proprioceptive feedback, something that organizes her nervous system. maybe it's communication — the only way she has to say 'this is too much' or 'i need help' or 'something is wrong.' maybe it's escape — the hitting so consumes everyone's attention that overwhelming demands disappear. maybe it's regulation — somehow the pain or the rhythm or the intensity calms something inside her that nothing else can reach. the behavior is functional. it works for her brain. but the way it works is destroying her body. she has calluses on her hands. she's had black eyes. she's had goose eggs on her forehead that take weeks to heal. she's knocked out a tooth. every time she's hurt, i feel like i've failed her. the behavior is functional, they keep telling me. so i can't just stop it. if i block the behavior without giving her another way to meet the need, the need doesn't go away. it just finds another outlet, often worse. she'll bite instead of hit. bang instead of slap. the energy has to go somewhere. i need to give her another way. not just stop her — that doesn't work and sometimes makes things worse. i need to give her something else she can do instead. something that meets the same need without the damage. something her body will accept as a substitute for the hitting. because she's not doing this to hurt herself. not on purpose. not as a goal. she's doing this because her brain has found a solution to an unbearable problem — the overwhelming sensation, the trapped communication, the stuck emotion, the unspeakable pain — and right now, hitting her head is the only solution she has. i need to give her better solutions. i need to understand what she's looking for — the sensory input, the communication, the escape, the regulation — and offer it in a form that doesn't leave bruises. that's what replacement behaviors are, they tell me. not just stopping the bad thing, but giving something better that works just as well. meeting the need through a different path. keeping her safe while honoring that the need is real. but how do i know what she needs? how do i find substitutes that actually work? how do i teach a new behavior when she can barely learn new skills? how do i keep her safe while we figure this out? where do i even start?

acceptedAnswer.text: SELF-INJURIOUS BEHAVIOR (SIB) / FUNCTIONAL REPLACEMENT BEHAVIOR TRAINING / FUNCTIONALLY EQUIVALENT RESPONSE DEVELOPMENT! Self-injurious behavior (SIB) refers to any behavior in which a person inflicts physical harm on their own body. In children, common forms include head hitting/banging, self-biting, self-scratching, hair pulling, and skin picking. SIB is conceptualized as functional behavior — it serves a purpose for the individual, typically falling into one or more of four functions: sensory/automatic (the behavior produces sensory input the nervous system seeks), escape/avoidance (the behavior terminates or delays aversive situations), attention/social (the behavior produces caregiver response), or tangible (the behavior results in access to preferred items/activities). Replacement behavior training identifies the function(s) the SIB serves and teaches alternative behaviors that serve the same function without causing harm. Effective replacement behaviors must be functionally equivalent (meeting the same need), physically easier or equally easy to perform, produce the same or faster results, and be acceptable in the child's environments. Materials support replacement behavior training by providing appropriate sensory alternatives, communication tools, regulation supports, and safe outlets for intense physical needs.

mainEntity.name: What is Self-Injurious Behavior (SIB) / Functional Replacement Behavior Training / Functionally Equivalent Response Development?

acceptedAnswer.text: Self-injurious behavior (SIB) refers to any behavior in which a person inflicts physical harm on their own body. In children, common forms include head hitting/banging, self-biting, self-scratching, hair pulling, and skin picking. SIB is conceptualized as functional behavior — it serves a purpose for the individual, typically falling into one or more of four functions: sensory/automatic (the behavior produces sensory input the nervous system seeks), escape/avoidance (the behavior terminates or delays aversive situations), attention/social (the behavior produces caregiver response), or tangible (the behavior results in access to preferred items/activities). Replacement behavior training identifies the function(s) the SIB serves and teaches alternative behaviors that serve the same function without causing harm. Effective replacement behaviors must be functionally equivalent (meeting the same need), physically easier or equally easy to perform, produce the same or faster results, and be acceptable in the child's environments. Materials support replacement behavior training by providing appropriate sensory alternatives, communication tools, regulation supports, and safe outlets for intense physical needs. Common signs include: Hitting head with hands, against surfaces, or with objects, Biting self — hands, arms, fingers, Scratching skin until marks or wounds appear, Hair pulling, Skin picking or wound picking.

mainEntity.name: How does a High-Intensity Sensory Tools help with self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Many SIB provides intense sensory input — the pressure of hitting, the deep proprioceptive feedback of impact, the organizing stimulation that overwhelming intensity provides. When SIB is sensory-driven, replacement materials must match that intensity. Light-touch alternatives won't substitute for head-banging. Heavy work activities, resistance tools, crash pads, squeeze machines, intense vibration, and high-pressure input offer the same deep, organizing stimulation without tissue damage. The key is intensity matching: if the child is hitting hard, they need something they can push hard against. If they're seeking impact, they need something they can crash into. These tools don't eliminate the sensory need — they meet it through safer channels. The body gets what it's looking for without getting hurt. Price: ₹500–5,000. DIY option: Create heavy work opportunities: pushing furniture, carrying heavy items, wall push-ups, jumping, crashing into couch cushions, squeezing stress balls forcefully. Use weighted blankets, compression clothing, or joint compressions. The key is matching the intensity the child's system is seeking. Safety: Sensory tools must be used correctly to be safe. Weighted items have duration and weight guidelines. Compression has limits. Work with an occupational therapist to determine appropriate intensity and duration for your child.

mainEntity.name: How does a Functional Communication Tools help with self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: When SIB communicates something — 'this is too much,' 'I need help,' 'something hurts,' 'I want that,' 'leave me alone' — the replacement behavior must be a form of communication that works as well or better. For children with limited speech, AAC devices, picture exchange systems, sign language, or simple gesture systems provide ways to say what the body has been screaming. The communication replacement must be: easier than the SIB, faster to get results, and consistently honored. If asking for a break takes 10 steps and hitting gets an instant break, hitting will continue. The communication alternative must be taught during calm times and prompted/honored immediately when distress appears. These tools give voice to needs that were trapped in the body. Price: ₹500–15,000. DIY option: Create simple communication cards: STOP, BREAK, HELP, HURTS, MORE, ALL DONE. Teach a simple sign (like 'help' or 'stop'). Use consistent single words or phrases that you honor immediately. The system doesn't need to be complex — it needs to work fast and be honored every time. Safety: Communication alternatives only work if they're honored. Every time the child uses the appropriate communication, they must get what they asked for (break, help, escape, item). If appropriate communication is ignored, they'll return to what works — the SIB.

mainEntity.name: How does a Safe Oral Motor Alternatives help with self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Self-biting is one of the most common forms of SIB, and it serves multiple possible functions: the deep proprioceptive input of jaw pressure, the sensory input on the skin, regulation through oral stimulation, or expression of distress. Safe chewing alternatives provide similar input through the mouth and jaw without tissue damage. Chewable jewelry, bite tubes, and textured chew tools give the jaw something to work against with significant resistance. Some children need very firm resistance (like rubber tubing); others need texture variation. The alternative must be immediately available when the urge appears — often worn on the body or kept within arm's reach at all times. Oral motor tools intercept the behavior by offering the mouth something safe to engage with. Price: ₹300–1,500. DIY option: Silicone teethers work for younger children. For older children, look for age-appropriate chewable jewelry or tools. Ensure items are made from safe, non-toxic materials and rated for oral use. Replace when worn or damaged. Keep multiple available in all settings. Safety: All oral motor tools must be safe for chewing — made from non-toxic, food-grade materials. Check for damage regularly as worn tools can become choking hazards. Size appropriately to prevent choking. Some children with severe SIB may damage even 'bite-proof' tools.

mainEntity.name: How does a Head Protection and Safe Impact help with self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: For head-hitting and head-banging specifically, two strategies work together: reducing harm when hitting occurs and providing safe alternatives for impact-seeking. Protective helmets reduce injury during episodes while replacement alternatives are being established. Simultaneously, safe impact activities — hitting drums, pounding clay, using punching bags, slamming hands on tables — give the proprioceptive impact feedback the nervous system may be seeking. The goal is not helmet-wearing forever but harm reduction while teaching alternatives. Some children seek the specific feedback of head impact; for them, activities that provide intense head/neck proprioception (inversions, head-down positions, joint compressions to head/neck) may address the underlying need. Protection buys time while replacement behaviors are learned. Price: ₹1,000–8,000. DIY option: Create pounding activities: playdough or clay to pound, pillows to punch, surfaces that can be hit safely. For protection, padded headbands or soft helmets designed for children with SIB. Work with OT on safe inversion activities and head/neck proprioception. Safety: Protective helmets are harm reduction, not solution. The goal is teaching replacements so protection becomes unnecessary. Consult medical professionals about appropriate protection that doesn't restrict or increase other risks. Some children escalate force when wearing helmets.

mainEntity.name: How does a Emotional Regulation and Expression Tools help with self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: SIB often occurs when emotions exceed the child's capacity to process or express them. The overwhelm becomes physical because it can't stay verbal or cognitive. Regulation tools — visual emotion scales, calm-down kits, breathing tools, fidgets, stress balls — and expression outlets — art materials, music, movement — provide ways to process intense emotion without self-harm. These work best when introduced and practiced during calm times, then prompted when escalation begins. The child learns: 'When I feel this way, I can do this instead.' Regulation tools won't stop SIB mid-episode, but they can intercept the escalation pathway if used early enough. They also build emotional capacity over time, reducing the frequency of overwhelming emotional states. Price: ₹200–2,000. DIY option: Create a calm-down kit: stress balls, fidgets, breathing prompts, emotion cards. Practice using these during calm times. Create visual scripts for regulation: 'When I'm upset, I can squeeze, breathe, ask for break.' Make art/music available as expression outlets. Safety: Regulation tools work for emotional-function SIB but not all SIB. If the function is primarily sensory or escape, regulation tools alone won't address the need. Function determines which replacements will work.

mainEntity.name: How does a Escape and Break Systems help with self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: When SIB functions to escape or avoid overwhelming situations — too many demands, sensory overload, social stress, pain — the replacement behavior must be an appropriate way to access escape. Break cards, 'help' signals, escape requests, and pre-taught break routines give children a way to exit overwhelming situations without hurting themselves. The critical requirement: the break must be honored every time it's requested appropriately. If the child has to hit their head three times before getting a break, they'll learn that hitting is necessary. If one break card gets instant break, the card will replace the hitting. Break systems must be available in all settings and immediately effective. This doesn't mean children never have to do hard things — it means they have appropriate ways to ask for relief. Price: ₹100–800. DIY option: Create simple break cards (can be as simple as a red card or the word BREAK). Practice using them when the child is calm. When the child uses the card, IMMEDIATELY provide a break — even briefly. Build from there. Consistency is everything. Safety: Escape systems only work if they work. If breaks aren't honored, children learn that appropriate requests are useless and return to what works: the SIB. Commitment to honoring break requests is prerequisite to this intervention.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Self Injurious Behavior (sib)
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Self-injurious behavior is functional behavior that meets a need. Effective intervention identifies the function(s) the SIB serves and teaches alternative behaviors that serve the same function without causing harm. Replacement behaviors must be functionally equivalent (meeting the same need), easier or equally easy to perform, equally or more efficient at producing the desired outcome, and acceptable across settings. Simply suppressing SIB without providing alternatives typically fails or produces worse alternatives. Function-based replacement is the evidence-based approach.

step.name: Understand the Problem
step.text: Self-injurious behavior (SIB) refers to any behavior in which a person inflicts physical harm on their own body. In children, common forms include head hitting/banging, self-biting, self-scratching, hair pulling, and skin picking. SIB is conceptualized as functional behavior — it serves a purpose for the individual, typically falling into one or more of four functions: sensory/automatic (the behavior produces sensory input the nervous system seeks), escape/avoidance (the behavior terminates or delays aversive situations), attention/social (the behavior produces caregiver response), or tangible (the behavior results in access to preferred items/activities). Replacement behavior training identifies the function(s) the SIB serves and teaches alternative behaviors that serve the same function without causing harm. Effective replacement behaviors must be functionally equivalent (meeting the same need), physically easier or equally easy to perform, produce the same or faster results, and be acceptable in the child's environments. Materials support replacement behavior training by providing appropriate sensory alternatives, communication tools, regulation supports, and safe outlets for intense physical needs. Self-injurious behavior is functional behavior that meets a need. Effective intervention identifies the function(s) the SIB serves and teaches alternative behaviors that serve the same function without causing harm. Replacement behaviors must be functionally equivalent (meeting the same need), easier or equally easy to perform, equally or more efficient at producing the desired outcome, and acceptable across settings. citation.name: Research on Functional Behavior Assessment and Replacement Behavior Training
citation.description: Applied Behavior Analysis research consistently demonstrates that self-injurious behavior serves functional purposes (sensory, escape, attention, tangible) and is most effectively addressed through functional behavior assessment identifying the maintaining variables, followed by training of functionally equivalent replacement behaviors. Replacement behaviors must meet the same need as the SIB, be equally or more efficient, and produce equivalent or better outcomes. Function-based interventions have significantly stronger evidence than non-function-based approaches.

citation.name: Pinnacle Blooms Network® Clinical Data
citation.description: 20M+ exclusive 1:1 therapy sessions with 97%+ measured improvement rate via GPT-OS® therapeutic system, including behavioral safety tracking

citation.name: Journal of Applied Behavior Analysis - FCT Research
citation.description: Functional Communication Training research demonstrates that teaching appropriate communication to replace problem behavior effectively reduces SIB when the communication serves the same function

citation.name: Sensory Processing and SIB Research
citation.description: Research links sensory processing differences to SIB and supports sensory-based interventions as components of comprehensive treatment plans

item.name: Functional Communication Tools
item.description: When SIB communicates something — 'this is too much,' 'I need help,' 'something hurts,' 'I want that,' 'leave me alone' — the replacement behavior must be a form of communication that works as well or better. For children with limited speech, AAC devices, picture exchange systems, sign language, or simple gesture systems provide ways to say what the body has been screaming. The communication replacement must be: easier than the SIB, faster to get results, and consistently honored. If asking for a break takes 10 steps and hitting gets an instant break, hitting will continue. The communication alternative must be taught during calm times and prompted/honored immediately when distress appears. These tools give voice to needs that were trapped in the body.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create simple communication cards: STOP, BREAK, HELP, HURTS, MORE, ALL DONE. Teach a simple sign (like 'help' or 'stop'). Use consistent single words or phrases that you honor immediately. The system doesn't need to be complex — it needs to work fast and be honored every time.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Communication alternatives only work if they're honored. Every time the child uses the appropriate communication, they must get what they asked for (break, help, escape, item). If appropriate communication is ignored, they'll return to what works — the SIB.

item.name: Safe Oral Motor Alternatives
item.description: Self-biting is one of the most common forms of SIB, and it serves multiple possible functions: the deep proprioceptive input of jaw pressure, the sensory input on the skin, regulation through oral stimulation, or expression of distress. Safe chewing alternatives provide similar input through the mouth and jaw without tissue damage. Chewable jewelry, bite tubes, and textured chew tools give the jaw something to work against with significant resistance. Some children need very firm resistance (like rubber tubing); others need texture variation. The alternative must be immediately available when the urge appears — often worn on the body or kept within arm's reach at all times. Oral motor tools intercept the behavior by offering the mouth something safe to engage with.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Silicone teethers work for younger children. For older children, look for age-appropriate chewable jewelry or tools. Ensure items are made from safe, non-toxic materials and rated for oral use. Replace when worn or damaged. Keep multiple available in all settings.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: All oral motor tools must be safe for chewing — made from non-toxic, food-grade materials. Check for damage regularly as worn tools can become choking hazards. Size appropriately to prevent choking. Some children with severe SIB may damage even 'bite-proof' tools.

item.name: Head Protection and Safe Impact
item.description: For head-hitting and head-banging specifically, two strategies work together: reducing harm when hitting occurs and providing safe alternatives for impact-seeking. Protective helmets reduce injury during episodes while replacement alternatives are being established. Simultaneously, safe impact activities — hitting drums, pounding clay, using punching bags, slamming hands on tables — give the proprioceptive impact feedback the nervous system may be seeking. The goal is not helmet-wearing forever but harm reduction while teaching alternatives. Some children seek the specific feedback of head impact; for them, activities that provide intense head/neck proprioception (inversions, head-down positions, joint compressions to head/neck) may address the underlying need. Protection buys time while replacement behaviors are learned.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create pounding activities: playdough or clay to pound, pillows to punch, surfaces that can be hit safely. For protection, padded headbands or soft helmets designed for children with SIB. Work with OT on safe inversion activities and head/neck proprioception.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Protective helmets are harm reduction, not solution. The goal is teaching replacements so protection becomes unnecessary. Consult medical professionals about appropriate protection that doesn't restrict or increase other risks. Some children escalate force when wearing helmets.

item.name: Emotional Regulation and Expression Tools
item.description: SIB often occurs when emotions exceed the child's capacity to process or express them. The overwhelm becomes physical because it can't stay verbal or cognitive. Regulation tools — visual emotion scales, calm-down kits, breathing tools, fidgets, stress balls — and expression outlets — art materials, music, movement — provide ways to process intense emotion without self-harm. These work best when introduced and practiced during calm times, then prompted when escalation begins. The child learns: 'When I feel this way, I can do this instead.' Regulation tools won't stop SIB mid-episode, but they can intercept the escalation pathway if used early enough. They also build emotional capacity over time, reducing the frequency of overwhelming emotional states.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a calm-down kit: stress balls, fidgets, breathing prompts, emotion cards. Practice using these during calm times. Create visual scripts for regulation: 'When I'm upset, I can squeeze, breathe, ask for break.' Make art/music available as expression outlets.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Regulation tools work for emotional-function SIB but not all SIB. If the function is primarily sensory or escape, regulation tools alone won't address the need. Function determines which replacements will work.

item.name: Escape and Break Systems
item.description: When SIB functions to escape or avoid overwhelming situations — too many demands, sensory overload, social stress, pain — the replacement behavior must be an appropriate way to access escape. Break cards, 'help' signals, escape requests, and pre-taught break routines give children a way to exit overwhelming situations without hurting themselves. The critical requirement: the break must be honored every time it's requested appropriately. If the child has to hit their head three times before getting a break, they'll learn that hitting is necessary. If one break card gets instant break, the card will replace the hitting. Break systems must be available in all settings and immediately effective. This doesn't mean children never have to do hard things — it means they have appropriate ways to ask for relief.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create simple break cards (can be as simple as a red card or the word BREAK). Practice using them when the child is calm. When the child uses the card, IMMEDIATELY provide a break — even briefly. Build from there. Consistency is everything.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Escape systems only work if they work. If breaks aren't honored, children learn that appropriate requests are useless and return to what works: the SIB. Commitment to honoring break requests is prerequisite to this intervention.

item.name: Attention and Connection Alternatives
item.description: Some SIB is maintained by the intense attention it produces — the immediate caregiver response, the connection in crisis, the focus that injury brings. This doesn't mean children are 'attention-seeking' manipulators — it means they have profound connection needs that aren't being met and have learned that crisis brings connection. Replacement involves two components: teaching appropriate attention-requesting (tapping shoulder, saying 'look,' using 'talk to me' cards) and massively increasing non-contingent attention (connection that isn't earned by crisis). When children receive abundant, predictable connection throughout the day, the need for crisis-based attention diminishes. Scheduled attention, predictable connection rituals, and responsive interaction for appropriate bids make SIB less necessary.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Schedule regular, predictable connection times (5-10 minutes multiple times daily). Respond immediately to appropriate attention requests. Create simple 'talk to me' or 'play with me' cards. Catch appropriate bids for attention and respond to them. Front-load connection rather than responding only to crisis.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: This approach should not mean ignoring SIB (which isn't safe) but rather decreasing its necessity by flooding the environment with non-contingent connection. The child should get abundant attention without SIB, making SIB less effective as an attention strategy.

item.name: Pain and Discomfort Communication
item.description: SIB sometimes communicates physical pain or discomfort that the child cannot otherwise express. Ear infections, headaches, dental pain, GI distress, constipation, menstrual cramps — pain that cannot be named often becomes pain that is expressed through self-injury. For non-verbal or minimally verbal children, pain communication tools are essential. Body charts where children can point to what hurts, pain scales adapted for developmental level, and specific 'something hurts' cards give children ways to tell us about pain. When we identify and treat the underlying pain, the SIB addressing that pain decreases. Pain-related SIB requires both communication tools AND medical investigation. The communication tool helps locate the pain; the medical team treats it.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a simple body chart (outline of body). Teach child to point to where something hurts. Create 'hurts' or 'owie' cards. When SIB increases suddenly, investigate possible pain sources: check ears, teeth, stomach. Track patterns that might indicate pain (time of day, after eating, etc.).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Sudden increases in SIB should always trigger investigation of possible pain or medical causes. Children with communication limitations are at higher risk for undiagnosed pain conditions. Medical evaluation is essential, not optional.

item.name: Environmental Modification Tools
item.description: Sometimes the most effective replacement for SIB is preventing the conditions that trigger it. Environmental modification tools reduce the triggers: noise-canceling headphones for auditory overwhelm, visual schedules for unpredictability, demand reduction during high-risk times, sensory diets that prevent dysregulation, and calm spaces for retreat. These materials don't replace the behavior directly — they reduce the need for it by keeping the nervous system regulated enough that SIB isn't triggered. This is prevention, not replacement, but it's essential. When we understand what triggers SIB (sensory overload, transitions, demands, changes, pain), we can modify the environment to reduce those triggers while teaching alternative responses.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Track SIB patterns to identify triggers. Reduce those triggers: noise-canceling headphones for sound, visual schedules for unpredictability, demand reduction during high-risk times, frequent sensory breaks, access to calm spaces. Modify before meltdown, not during.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Environmental modification is one component of a comprehensive plan. Reducing triggers doesn't teach replacement skills — it buys time and reduces episodes while replacements are being taught. Both are needed.

socialMedia.caption: When the body hurts itself to cope, we give it other ways. SIB happens for a reason — the behavior works. Our job is to offer safer alternatives that work just as well. 9 materials for replacement behaviors: high-intensity sensory tools, communication systems, oral motor alternatives, safe impact options, regulation tools, escape systems, connection alternatives, pain communication, and environmental modifications. The need is real. The path can change. Save for safer alternatives. 🔖 #SIBReplacement #ReplacementBehaviors #PinnacleBlooms
